title: Uva-ursi stanfordiana
intro: Uva-ursi stanfordiana, also known as kinnikinnick or bearberry, is a species of flowering plant in the heath family, Ericaceae. This plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, particularly in treating urinary tract infections and reducing inflammation, due to its ability to inhibit the growth of bacteria such as E. coli and reduce oxidative stress. The bioactive constituents of Uva-ursi stanfordiana include arbutin, hydroquinone, and ursolic acid, which have been shown to possess antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties. Uva-ursi stanfordiana is commonly used in herbal preparations such as teas, tinctures, and infusions, often in combination with other herbs like juniper and uva ursi, to create remedies for urinary issues and other health concerns.

properties: Uva-ursi stanfordiana is known to possess several therapeutic properties, primarily due to its high content of arbutin and hydroquinone compounds. These compounds have been found to have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, making the plant useful in treating urinary tract infections and other infections. Additionally, Uva-ursi stanfordiana has been traditionally used to treat digestive issues and as a natural diuretic. However, it is essential to note that Uva-ursi stanfordiana should be used under medical supervision due to potential interactions with other medications.
constituents: Uva-ursi stanfordiana is known to contain various bioactive compounds, including arbutin, ursolic acid, and betulinic acid. These active constituents contribute to the plant's medicinal properties. Arbutin, in particular, has been shown to have antibacterial and anti-inflammatory effects. Ursolic acid has been found to possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, while betulinic acid has been reported to exhibit antiviral and antibacterial activities. The combination of these compounds may contribute to the plant's traditional use in treating urinary tract infections and other health conditions.
preparations: Uva-ursi stanfordiana preparations are not commonly documented in modern literature.
